explanation: Incidental learning is learning that happens without any intent to learn, so not warning participants about the later test is the defining setup. Intentional learning is the opposite condition (participants are told they will be tested).

Free recall means producing the studied items in any order with no cues provided. Cued recall would supply a hint at test, and implicit/explicit memory describe the type of memory, not the test format.

Telling participants they will be tested and then asking them to deliberately retrieve the list makes it a conscious, intentional retrieval, which is the definition of an explicit memory test. Recognition is a trap: it is one specific explicit test format, but the stem never says the words are re-presented for a yes/no judgement.

Memory has three stages: encoding (interpreting and committing information), storage (retaining it), and retrieval (getting it back). Storage is therefore the retention interval between presentation of the stimuli and the test; options A and B describe encoding.

To isolate storage you must vary only what happens during the retention interval, that is, after presentation and before the test. Manipulating the presentation phase (D) targets encoding, and providing recall cues (C) targets retrieval.

The dot probe task measures attentional bias toward emotional stimuli; it is not a memory test at all. Word stem completion (implicit), cued recall (explicit) and serial position effects are all standard retrieval measures.

The serial position curve is U-shaped: early items benefit from primacy (rehearsal into long-term memory) and late items from recency (still in short-term store), so middle items get neither boost and are recalled worst.

Better recall of the last items on an immediate test is the recency effect. Serial position effect is the trap: it is the umbrella term for the whole U-shaped curve (primacy plus recency), not the end-of-list advantage specifically.

Recency depends on the final items still sitting in the short-term store, so lengthening the retention interval (for example by inserting a filled delay before the test) wipes it out. Preventing rehearsal mainly attacks primacy, not recency.

The modal (multi-store) model of sensory store to short-term store to long-term store is Atkinson and Shiffrin (1968). Craik and Lockhart (1972) proposed levels of processing, and Tulving and Thomson proposed encoding specificity.

The modal model routes everything through short-term memory into long-term memory, so it predicts that damaging short-term memory should block new long-term learning. Neuropsychological cases with badly impaired short-term memory but intact long-term learning break that serial chain. Options B and D are not challenges: limited short-term capacity is a claim the model itself makes.

In Craik and Lockhart's levels of processing, depth increases from structural to phonological to semantic, so processing meaning produces the most durable trace. Perceptual processing is the shallowest level.
The standard criticism is circularity: depth of processing is defined by how well the material is later remembered, and there is no independent measure of depth, so the theory cannot be falsified.

A cue helps only to the extent that the information it carries was part of the trace laid down at encoding, that is, overlap between the cue and what was encoded (encoding specificity). Interference and decay explain forgetting, not why cues help.

That sentence is Tulving and Thomson's statement of the encoding specificity principle: retrieval succeeds when the cue matches what was actually stored at encoding. Context-dependent memory is a consequence of this principle, not the principle itself.

Retroactive inhibition is new learning interfering with the retrieval of older material, so it is an interference account of forgetting, not a decay or delay account (decay says traces fade with time on their own).
Proactive inhibition is old learning interfering forward in time with new learning. Retroactive is the mirror image (new interferes with old), which is the trap here.

Keppel's asymmetry: proactive interference is restricted, old learning only impairs new learning when the material is similar, whereas retroactive interference is general, new learning impairs old learning broadly. Option B is a near-duplicate that drops the similarity restriction, which is exactly the discriminating detail.

Jost's law is the formal statement that of two equally strong memories the older one decays more slowly, which is what gives the forgetting curve its decelerating (roughly power/logarithmic) shape. The distractors are empirical effects, not accounts of the curve's mathematical form.

Lansdale and Baguley's fragmentation account treats a memory as a set of traces, so the probability of correct recall is a function of the proportion of correct traces available at test. The other options name principles about cues and interference rather than a quantity that predicts recall probability.
